text: I usually have toast with butter and jam for brekkie. Sometimes, if I'm feeling fancy, I'll make myself some scrambled eggs or an omelette with smoked salmon – my granddaughter made me try them once, and now I'm hooked! A cuppa tea always accompanies it, of course. Nothing too terribly exciting, really, but simple comfort food does the job nicely.

I usually take my midday meal around noon, dear. It's a lovely break before the afternoon slump sets in. My grandkids sometimes join me for sandwiches and a chat while we watch telly together; other days I might pop out into the local café for something more substantial – maybe soup and a sandwich or leftovers from last night's dinner reheated in the microwave. If I'm feeling peckish later, I'll have a slice of cake or a biscuit from a packet left over from one of our weekend baking sessions. Nothing too fancy, really – just simple comfort food to give me enough energy to face whatever the rest of the day throws my way!

I try to be in bed by 10 o'clock, but sometimes I might slip into bed a bit later than that, especially if I'm watching something on telly before winding down. Still, my body likes me to stick to an 11 pm cut-off these days, so that gives me a decent night's sleep.

I think my key to a happy and fulfilling retirement is finding a balance between keeping busy and taking some time for myself. For me, that starts with getting out into nature – whether that's tending to my vegetable garden, going for a walk along our local park, or simply sitting outside in the morning enjoying the fresh air. It really helps set a positive tone for the rest of the day. After that, I try to establish a bit of a routine. A cup of tea, perhaps a biscuit, and catching up on the news before settling in for a spot of reading. I find solace in immersing myself in history books or biographies; there's something comforting about escaping reality for a while and exploring different eras and lives. Exercise isn't always easy, mind you! My body creaky joints aren't what they used to be, so I stick to gentle activities like yoga or stretching exercises. But even those little bits make all the difference. And, of course, spending time with loved ones is vital too – whether it's my grandkids dropping by unannounced or meeting friends for a quiet coffee. It sounds simple, maybe, but for an older chap like myself, these tiny moments add up to making life feel rich and meaningful

Networking within our organisation is quite simple, really. As a former secretary who worked her way up, I'm still remembered fondly by colleagues for my reliability and ability to get work done efficiently. When we're discussing projects or need assistance with something, people usually know exactly where to find me. Of course, keeping records and being organised has always been key – I'd jot down names, dates, and other relevant information so everyone could refer back to them later. It's amazing how far those little notes have helped us all along the way!

Well, I'm fortunate enough now to have more freedom since retiring, so my work-from-home arrangement is quite different from what most people might be used to these days! However, during my working life, managing distractions was crucial for me. I found that having a dedicated workspace helped me create some discipline. My ideal setup included a large desk near a window with plenty of natural light – I loved watching the garden come alive while still being productive. A cup of tea nearby kept me company throughout the morning. When working remotely, it was essential to establish routines similar to those I had in an office. This meant setting regular breaks every hour or two, taking short walks outside, and keeping my computer screen at eye level. A strict separation between personal space and work was vital too; that way, once I left the room, I could relax and recharge before returning to focused work. Using noise-cancelling headphones or playing calming music allowed me to block out any internal chatter or external disturbances. And let's not forget staying hydrated and energised – biscuits were always readily available! It really comes down to establishing your own system, finding what works best for you personally and sticking to it. It took trial-and-error, but eventually, I got into a rhythm that served me well during

I find video calls a bit challenging, to be honest. As much as I love staying in touch with my loved ones, I'm still getting used to having these devices and navigating them properly. My grandsons have been very helpful, teaching me how to work my iPad, but sometimes I feel like there's so many options and buttons to press – it all gets a bit overwhelming! Still, we make an effort to catch up regularly via video call whenever circumstances allow; it feels nice to see each other's faces even though it's not exactly what I'd consider "natural" conversation style.

Well, you know, since retiring, I think I'd found a nice balance between freedom and discipline. For me, having a regular daily routine helps a lot – I set aside dedicated blocks of time for my hobbies like gardening and reading, which gives me something to look forward to. Plus, staying involved with our local community centre and volunteering there every week provides a sense of purpose. And, of course, keeping busy is key! I make sure to plan out my weekdays fairly thoroughly, breaking them into manageable chunks with breaks throughout. It sounds simple, but really does work for me. Having some flexibility still allows me to take spontaneous moments whenever needed. Overall, finding that sweet spot where structure isn't too rigid but enough to keep me going seems to be what works best for me now.

I think one has got to be flexible, dear. As a seasoned admin professional, I've seen my fair share of changes in priorities come along. It's essential to remain calm and assess what needs shifting first. For instance, during my time in education, we'd have to adjust our lesson plans according to weather conditions – so rain meant extra indoor games instead! But seriously, when faced with changing priorities, I take stock of everything, delegate where possible, communicate clearly with colleagues, and adapt accordingly. Sometimes, it requires making sacrifices, but being able to roll with the punches is key. And remember, someone else might need your support too, so prioritising those needs comes into play as well. A bit like juggling multiple balls; you must know which ones to catch first!

Well, you know, I think being a strong communicator is key. When there's an issue, I'll speak up directly and clearly, explaining my concerns and why I believe something should be done differently. It might sound too blunt, but I'm not one for beating about the bush! I make sure everyone involved understands where I stand, even if we don't always agree. Of course, I also like to have all relevant facts and figures ready to support me, so I feel more confident making informed decisions. And if needed, I will politely push back against suggestions that aren't feasible or wouldn't achieve our shared objectives – I'd rather take a little extra time now to find the best solution later on.

When I feel like I'm being misunderstood, I try to take a step back and think clearly about what's really going on. Sometimes, I'll ask people directly how they perceive me or my intentions, so we can have an open conversation. It's amazing how much clarity comes from talking things out. I also remind myself that I might be bringing different perspectives or cultural backgrounds to the table, which can sometimes lead to misunderstandings. Being honest and explaining where I come from helps others see that I'm coming from a place of kindness and respect. If all else fails, I recall something my late husband used to say – "Patience and curiosity get you further than rushing through life." He'd always tell me to breathe deeply, listen more closely, and consider multiple viewpoints before reacting. His advice has stuck with me over the years, and I find it helps me navigate tricky conversations with greater ease.

I love spending time with my grandkids! During family gatherings, we usually have a big spread of food, including all sorts of delicious dishes like roast meats, vegetables, and desserts. My granddaughter is quite the baker herself, so there's always something tasty to try. We spend most of our time chatting and laughing together, sharing stories, and having a lovely time catching up. Sometimes we'll play games or watch a movie together, but generally it's just nice, relaxed conversation and enjoying each other's company. It brings me great joy to be surrounded by such wonderful people who make every gathering feel like an event.

To be honest, I'm not too keen on these platforms. As a simple person who enjoys staying connected with loved ones rather than networking professionals, I find all the fuss around LinkedIn quite overwhelming. My son says he likes connecting me there because "Grandma" has lots of followers! However, I prefer keeping my online presence tidy and manageable; I only have one account where I post updates about local events, like our village fete or charity fundraisers we're involved in. Really, that's about it. I'd much rather focus on more meaningful interactions – talking to people face-to-face or having a chat via phone.

I think I'd always try to be honest and direct, while still being considerate of how my words might affect others. If someone asks me a personal question, I'll usually give them an open and straightforward response, even if it's difficult. But before speaking, I take a moment to gather my thoughts, make sure I'm choosing the right words, and consider whether there's something else I could say instead – which is helpful in avoiding any unintended hurtful comments. It's all about finding that balance between candour and kindness, really.

I'm a bit of a straight-shooter, so I try to be honest with myself first. If someone's pushing me into something I don't feel like doing, I'll say no politely and explain why. But if we're talking amongst friends, I'd rather have an open conversation about my concerns before making any decisions together. I believe people generally respect your decision once you've had a chance to think about it and share your thoughts. Of course, there are exceptions – if someone's being unreasonable or hurtful, I wouldn't hesitate to stand up for what I believe is right.

I try to be quite cautious when dealing with those sorts of advertisements. You know, all these tracking cookies and whatnot seem like a bit too much for me. When I'm browsing websites or watching something on my tablet, I'll usually just close any pop-ups immediately, unless there seems to be some genuine content worth looking into. And even then, I find myself getting frustrated because I never feel like I fully trust where my personal data is going once it gets shared with advertisers. Still, I suppose it's progress – we're living in an increasingly digital world now!

Well, I must admit, getting used to all these modern contraptions is a bit tricky for me! To be honest, most of my browsing is done using the web version of my favourite websites because that way I know exactly what I'm seeing. But sometimes, if I need something specific, like checking train times or looking up recipes, I'll open a separate tab for that particular task. And of course, I always make sure to close any unnecessary ones once I've finished with them – there's nothing worse than having too many cluttered tabs taking up space! My son does try to teach me how to organise everything more efficiently, but I think he knows better left to figure out his mum's quirks. Sometimes, though, even he gets frustrated with me asking him to explain something again... Oh dear, perhaps one of those days we'll sort it all out properly!

I try to ignore them really, unless they're directed at me personally or my work. You'd be surprised how much time those sorts of people take up, and I'm sure there are far more productive uses for your time. If someone is being abusive or threatening, I report them to the relevant authorities straight away – Facebook or Twitter will sort out the rest, usually. But generally speaking, a bit of silence seems to work best against negativity; ignoring it tends to deprive them of the reaction they crave.

Well, to be honest, I'm a bit frustrated when my video call isn't quite cooperating! My son usually sorts me out, he's very handy with these things. If I haven't got him nearby, I'll try calling his number first before we set up a call so he can come over and sort us out. Sometimes, I find myself getting a bit flustered, but I manage to take a deep breath and remember that it's only a minor inconvenience. It does make me think about how much more convenient modern technology has made our lives...

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned one, I suppose, but I find writing everything down helps me stay organised. I have a big notebook where I list all my due dates for each bill, including utilities, council tax, and whatnot. It's always tucked away in my bag so I never forget. Then every month, I sit down with my calendar and make sure I've got enough money set aside before making any payments. My grandkids like helping me sort out our joint expenses too – we work together until we're satisfied everyone has been covered. And, of course, having regular automatic payments makes it easier still. Still, there's nothing quite like seeing those cheques bounce, is there?! Ha ha!

I try to be honest with myself first – is there something I haven't used recently enough to justify keeping it, or am I holding onto it out of habit or nostalgia? If so, a quick thought about what would happen if I cancelled might give me some clarity. Plus, reviewing my bank statements helps too; if someone has been charged regularly, even if nothing seems wrong visually, I'll think twice before cancelling. Occasionally, having a chat with one of my grandkids who knows how much I value being frugal will encourage me to double-check whether any subscription really feels worth keeping!

I'd say I'm a very deliberate and thoughtful shopper when it comes to making big-ticket purchases. Before buying something like a replacement washing machine, I'll always consider whether we really need one and what will be the best option for us. If there is only one brand available, I might opt for the most affordable choice rather than going for top-of-the-line model. But once I feel fairly certain, I start researching different options, comparing prices, features, and reviews. It helps me make an informed decision and ensures we're getting value for our money. Sometimes my son likes to joke that I spend too much time deliberating! Still, someone has to look out for Granny's wallet, after all.

Well, I think I'm a bit of a thrifty soul, even now that I am retired! When deciding whether to buy something new or second-hand, I always try to weigh my options carefully. If what I need is readily available and reasonably priced, I tend towards going for the cheaper option unless there's some particular reason why a brand-new one would be preferable – perhaps because it has all sorts of bells and whistles that might make a difference. But generally speaking, saving money is very appealing to me! Nowadays, I must admit I find myself more drawn to browsing charity shops and auctions for bargains rather than scouring markets or estate agents. It's nice to give old items another chance and reduce waste, isn't it? But ultimately, the decision comes down to how much I'll actually end up using the item and whether I have space for it in my little flat. And of course, making sure it's clean and works properly before handing over my hard-earned cash is essential!

I think it's a habit I developed early on, really. Growing up during wartime rationing, we didn't have much money, so waste was seen as a luxury we couldn't afford. My mother always said "a penny saved is a penny earned" – it stuck with me. Even now, I'm very mindful of my spending. We'd go to charity shops and markets instead of buying brand-new things, which taught us how to make do with what we had. And my late husband always encouraged our kids to save a portion of their earnings; he'd say it was essential for building security. When we got married, we created a joint budget together, making sure every pound counted. It wasn't always easy, mind you! But we prioritised saving and investing in our future, including putting aside some each month into an ISA. Nowadays, even though we're retired, we still stick to our budgets closely. If something isn't absolutely necessary, I'll put it on hold until later, like those impulse buys that seem tempting at first but aren't worth diverting from our savings targets. The saying goes, "you should live below your means," and I believe that's true – there's more to happiness than throwing money away on frivolous expenses.

I try to take advantage of them whenever I can! You know, my mother always taught me "use what's on sale" – a bit of an old-fashioned motto, perhaps, but it still sticks. When I'm out buying essentials like groceries or cleaning supplies, I make sure to check the prices first and grab those items when they're discounted. And during Christmas, I love browsing the shops for bargains on gifts for the grandkids. It's all part of being thrifty, isn't it? That said, I do have to be mindful not to rush into purchases unnecessarily. Sometimes we'll end up bringing something home only to find it breaks before the winter is even over... But overall, I think keeping an eye out for deals makes our little household more financially secure, which is reassuring, really.

Portion control is quite simple for me. It's all about being mindful of my body and what works best for my lifestyle. Since I'm a bit older now, I try to eat smaller portions throughout the day rather than having big meals. And of course, I always make sure to include plenty of fruits and vegetables in my diet – they're so essential for keeping me going! My grandkids say I'm still very thrifty with food, even though we live far apart, which makes sense given where I come from. A penny saved is a penny earned, after all! Now, let me tell you, there have been some ups and downs with managing my weight over the years, but overall, moderation has served me fairly well.

I think a more traditional one-on-one session is best suited for me. I like having someone to talk to who has some professional training, but I'm not keen on group sessions where everyone's talking all the time. It feels too much like being stuck in a meeting! A steady conversation pace seems more comforting for my personality type. Plus, sometimes when people say these big words and jargon during groups, it makes me feel a bit lost – I prefer clarity and simple language. If we could sit comfortably and chat quietly, I'd appreciate that greatly.

Preventing burnout has become more relevant for me since retiring a couple of years ago. For someone like me who's always been quite driven and dedicated to my work, it's taken some getting used to slowing down a bit. But I think the key is setting realistic limits and prioritising what's truly important. For me, it starts with establishing a gentle morning routine – waking up feeling refreshed and having plenty of time to myself before the rest of the world wakes up. A nice cuppa, maybe some tending to my garden, and I'm set for the day. Of course, everyone's different, so finding your own rhythm is essential. I make sure to take breaks throughout the day too – perhaps going for a walk or chatting with neighbours. And while I still love staying connected with loved ones, I try not to be too available all the time. Having some downtime allows me to recharge and focus on meaningful interactions rather than being drained by constant notifications. It's funny how much our bodies respond to these little habits. By taking control of my daily routine and making conscious choices about what fills my free time, I feel far better equipped to manage the demands of everyday life. As my grandmother used to say, "Patience and curiosity" have served me well!

To be honest, getting a decent night's sleep has always been quite crucial for me, even in my younger years. Since retiring, I feel like I have more freedom to focus on establishing routines that suit me best. So, first thing is setting myself an achievable bedtime – usually around 10 pm sharp. Then, before bed, I try to wind down with some calming activities such as browsing gentle nature documentaries on telly or perhaps flipping through the pages of a novel until my eyelids start feeling heavy. It helps signal to my brain that it's time to relax. I avoid screens entirely afterwards, so no phones, tablets, laptops; nothing stimulating before lights out! A warm cup of herbal tea might come into play too, chamomile being one favourite choice. And, of course, keeping my room nice and cool – a bit chilly actually seems to work better for me. Just simple habits really, but it makes all the difference. I find that when done regularly, these little rituals become second-nature. Now, I'm able to wake up feeling refreshed, ready to tackle whatever the day throws our way.

I try to mix it up quite a bit, really. As much as I love my morning strolls round the block, I find I need something more stimulating later in the day. So, during those dips where I feel sluggish, usually around 2 o'clock after lunch, I'll put on some music and have a quick tidy of the living room – gets me moving and feeling like I'm accomplishing something, even if it is just clearing out the clutter! Sometimes, if the weather permits, I might take a walk along the nearby canal path; there's always someone interesting to chat with, and the fresh air does wonders for me. If I've got an afternoon appointment or meeting, I'll make sure to plan a longer walk before then so I'm energised. It helps me mentally prepare too – clears my head and gets me focused for whatever lies ahead. When grandkids come visit, we'll often go on little outings together – parks, museums, or simply exploring our local area. Those breaks are lovely, getting them outside and involved in nature or history is great for all of us! In the evenings, once everyone's tucked away, I might squeeze in some stretching exercises while watching telly or having dinner – never felt the need to join any gym

I think I evaluate them based on how much effort is required and whether it seems worthwhile. If something requires a lot of fuss and bother, like setting up some sort of complicated system, I'm likely to be hesitant unless someone shows me exactly what I need to do and explains it clearly. I'd rather have everything laid out before me so I know where I stand. For example, my grandkids helped me set up the video calling thingy on my tablet – now we chat regularly, which is lovely. But if someone had said "oh, let me show you," and tried to rush me into it without taking the time to explain properly, I might have been put off. It's all about finding that balance between being open to trying new things and feeling secure enough to trust that it'll work out okay. Does make sense?

I think incorporating feedback is crucial, really. When someone takes the trouble to give me something specific to say about my work or actions, it shows they're invested in seeing improvements happen. The key is to listen attentively and ask clarifying questions if needed, make sure I fully grasp what was said, and respond thoughtfully. It's a two-way process, isn't it? Feedback should be used constructively – neither to tear people down nor solely focus on pointing out mistakes, but rather to encourage growth and enhance overall performance. If there's an issue mentioned, I try to acknowledge it, reflect upon why it occurred, identify any patterns or root causes, and devise strategies for preventing similar issues moving forward. By putting myself in others' shoes, considering different viewpoints, being open-minded, and showing willingness to adapt, we can build trust and strengthen our relationships. If it feels like it'll genuinely improve outcomes, I'm more likely to accept changes suggested by feedback and even take ownership of implementing them. And afterwards, staying in touch to check how those suggestions have worked out helps us all learn together, adjust course corrections where necessary, and celebrate successes along the way.

I think my approach is quite methodical, really. I like to take a step-by-step approach whenever faced with an issue or puzzle. Breaking it down into smaller parts helps me understand each component better and find connections between them. It's rather like tending to my garden – you need to consider factors like soil, sunlight, water, and pests before tackling any actual planting. Once those basics are sorted, you're more likely to end up with something lovely! When applied to complex problems, that steady pace allows me to gather information, weigh options, and come to a conclusion. Of course, sometimes there might be twists along the way, but generally speaking, it works for me.

I find writing things down helps me process my thoughts better. Sometimes I make notes during meetings or while watching a documentary, so I can look back later and remember what was said or discussed. It's like taking an audio recording, but written form is more manageable for me. Writing it out gets everything straight in my mind and makes sense of all those bits and pieces floating about. Plus, if someone asks me something again months down the line, having some jotted-down notes handy saves us both time!

I think I'm fortunate to have had a relatively predictable work-life balance throughout my career, which has allowed me to focus on building strong relationships and staying organized. However, now that I'm retired, I find myself having more free time to reflect on how I'd like to spend it and what I could improve upon. To be honest, one area where I feel like there's room for growth is becoming more digitally literate. As someone who wasn't exactly tech-savvy even before retirement, I struggle with keeping up-to-date with all these new gadgets and software. My son helps me out occasionally, so I appreciate his guidance, but I wish I felt more confident using digital tools to manage my daily life. Another thing I'd love to tackle is finding more time for creative pursuits – perhaps taking painting classes or joining an arts course. While I used to dabble in crafting and baking, I haven't done much of those hobbies since retiring. It feels like such a shame, because I miss the sense of accomplishment and relaxation I got from creating something with my hands. Lastly, I suppose another area for personal growth would be being more proactive about health checks. With my husband passing away six years ago, I've become rather lax about scheduling regular check-ups. Although,

I think my approach is quite simple, really. It's all about taking a deep breath, being honest with myself, and asking why. You know, people always say "new experiences lead to personal growth," but what does that even mean to me? So, I try to dig beneath the surface, figure out the underlying reasons behind something. What am I hoping to gain? Is there someone else involved who might be able to guide me through it? Once I have those answers, I'll usually take tiny steps towards exploring the situation. Maybe start small – attending a local event, having an open conversation with someone – rather than diving straight into something overwhelming. And I'm prepared to walk away whenever I feel uncertain or uncomfortable; it's better to lose a little momentum now than risk getting stuck in something that isn't serving me. It sounds rather sensible, perhaps, but trust me, it works! Patience and curiosity seem to serve me reasonably well so far. As I said before, I believe that gets you further in life than rushing through things.

I make sure to carve out a bit each morning before getting into my usual routine. It helps me unwind and sets a peaceful tone for the rest of the day. Plus, being an early riser, I'm most alert during these quieter hours. My grandkids sometimes join me for breakfast while we catch up on current events together; it's lovely bonding time, really! After our chats, I usually settle in with a cuppa and some reading – be it history books, novels, or even a magazine. Sometimes I'll listen to audiobooks too, which is quite nice when I have other chores to attend to later in the day.

I usually find out about new authors or genres through word-of-mouth recommendations from my book club members or friends who have similar tastes. We discuss what we're currently reading and sometimes recommend books based on those conversations. Occasionally, I'll pick a title that catches my eye while browsing through newspapers or magazines, like The Times Literary Supplement or The Guardian Book Review section. Sometimes, too, I stumble upon an author or genre through radio programmes like the BBC's Front Row or Desert Island Discs – they always seem to know how to pitch an interesting story. And of course, I'm rather fond of a good publisher's website where I might come across something intriguing to read. But generally speaking, I think the most effective way is still getting advice from people I trust – fellow readers!

I love listening to music! When I'm enjoying a favourite album or artist, I'll usually sit quietly and let my mind wander. Sometimes I might think about memories associated with certain songs or artists. It's almost like having a conversation with an old friend – it feels comforting and familiar. Occasionally, I might find myself humming along or tapping my feet, even though I know nobody else is there to hear me! But most of all, I simply appreciate the soundscapes and melodies; it helps create a peaceful atmosphere for relaxation.

I must admit, I'm a bit of an old-fashioned listener, still prefer books myself, but I have taken to podcasting and audiobooks since my husband passed away. It's nice to be able to multitask while keeping him company during his daily commute. I find some subjects more engaging as audio than others; history is fascinating, and nature documentaries work beautifully narrated. However, I sometimes struggle to focus if there's too much chatter between episodes or distractions like ads – I appreciate being able to turn them off whenever needed!

I love staying active! You know, people might think being a retiree is all relaxation and lounging around, but I'm quite the opposite. I find joy in keeping my body moving, even if it's just taking short strolls around the block or doing some gentle stretching exercises. My grandkids always say I have boundless energy! Gardening is another favourite pastime of mine – there's something so satisfying about getting your hands dirty and watching those little seeds grow into lovely blooms. And of course, I adore spending time outdoors, whether it's tending to my vegetable patch or simply sitting in the sun-drenched backyard. It's wonderful to be able to breathe fresh air and connect with nature like that. When the weather permits, we usually take our daily walk along the nearby canal path; it's such a peaceful way to spend an hour or two. Sometimes we'll stop at one of the cafes near the lock gates for a nice cuppa and a chat. Those moments remind me how lucky I am to still feel young enough to appreciate these simple pleasures. It's amazing what gets us going sometimes, isn't it? As my late husband used to say, "Get out more!" He was spot on – getting outside does wonders for the soul.

I find solitude rather lovely, actually. As a morning person, my best time is usually between 7 am and 11 am, before the world wakes up fully. It's so peaceful during those hours. I'll have a cup of tea, sit quietly, and perhaps read something I'm enjoying – either history books, which fascinate me, or some gentle novels. Occasionally, I might take a walk out into our little garden; there's something therapeutic about being surrounded by nature even if only for a bit. It helps me unwind and set myself up for the rest of the day. Of course, sometimes I think about all the people who matter most, like grandkids, husband, and close friends – but generally speaking, I feel content enough in my own company.

I love exploring different cuisines! When trying something new, I always think "what's the story behind this dish?" You know, where does it come from, what inspired it... all those sorts of things. It adds a whole extra layer to enjoying food. My grandkids say I'm like a bit of a food historian, always asking about the origins and history of dishes we're having. If it's an unfamiliar one, I'll usually read up on some information beforehand so I have an idea of what to expect. But even that isn't always necessary – sometimes just giving it a go is half the fun! And yes, I must admit, my husband used to tease me that I was very particular about ingredients and presentation. Still am! Old habits die hard, I suppose. What really gets me excited, though, is seeing how people adapt and put their own spin on traditional dishes. The way they combine flavors and techniques is absolutely fascinating. Nowadays, since being widowed, I find myself cooking more for company – either it might be our kids coming over or hosting little gatherings for friends who live nearby. They always seem to appreciate my efforts, which makes it worthwhile. Of course, there's nothing quite like serving homemade food to make someone feel welcome.

I think having a mix of different hobbies is key to keeping my free time interesting. For example, some weeks I'll spend a lot of time tending to my vegetable garden, which I find really therapeutic. Other weeks I might be more focused on getting into reading historical novels or writing down our family history – my late husband was always keen on preserving those sorts of things, so now I like to carry on his work. And then there's baking for my grandkids; they love coming round for Sunday lunches and we have great fun experimenting with all sorts of tasty treats together. Really, I believe it's essential to balance out quieter moments with social ones – being part of a local book club helps fill that bill nicely!

I'm quite particular about keeping track of my free time. I have a little notebook where I jot down all sorts of things like what I'd like to read next, upcoming events at the local community centre, reminders for birthdays and anniversaries... really, everything that comes to mind. It helps me feel more organised and ensures I make the most of my downtime. My grandsons actually find it rather amusing how much I love filling those pages!

I find that keeping a planner is very helpful for staying organised. It's something my mother taught me when I was younger, and even now, I still carry one with me wherever I go. Although nowadays, most of what I jot down gets done via notes on my trusty little iPhone – a bit more modern than an actual notebook, isn’t it? But seriously, having some form of record seems essential; it allows me to review progress, make reminders, and cross-off completed tasks. My grandkids always tease me because they think I'm so tidy, which makes them happy! Of course, I wouldn't have been able to manage all these responsibilities during my working days without a system like that. In terms of digital tools, I try to stick to simple ones that work effectively rather than getting bogged down in complicated software. A calendar app where I can set recurring events and add comments helps with scheduling. And yes, I know there’s loads of different options available, but sometimes simplicity wins out. Oh, I almost forgot – my granddaughter has helped me download those fantastic voice assistant features on my device. Now, whenever someone wants information, we simply ask her to tell Alexa...

I like starting my morning routine quite early. As a morning person, I usually try to be out of bed by 6:30 am. The first thing I do is have a cup of tea while checking my emails and messages on my phone – helps me catch up on any important notifications. Then, I take some time to plan out my day, making sure I'm prepared for whatever comes up. Before heading out, I'll pack a little breakfast – sometimes toast or cereal, maybe some fruit if I feel fancy! It's lovely having some quiet time to myself to collect my thoughts before tackling whatever lies ahead. Plus, getting an hour or two into my daily activity always seems to give me a nice boost of energy.

I've always been keen on being mindful of our energy usage since we moved into this lovely old house. It's a bit draughty, so I make sure to turn down the heating when I'm out during the day and wear warm layers indoors. We have double glazing now, which has made a big difference, but there's still room for improvement. In fact, my granddaughter helped me assess where heat is escaping – it was mainly coming from one window, so we got some draft excluders for that. We try to unplug everything when not in use, like televisions and computers. And I only run full loads in the washing machine and dishwasher. Simple changes, really! My late husband used to tease me about how frugal I am, but he'd be pleased to know we're reducing waste even now, all these years later. Of course, I also make sure our boiler isn't too old; we had an ECO scheme last winter that gave us free insulation checks and advice on upgrading. All those little tweaks add up, don't they? It's funny, though – what started as just wanting to save money became more about enjoying the peace and quiet while watching nature programmes in front of the fire...

I'm still a bit hesitant with all these new-fangled gadgets, but my grandkids have been pestering me to upgrade our home. They say I should be more tech-savvy, so we've got an Amazon Echo thingy set up here now. It's alright, helps me keep track of what's going on at home while I'm out – reminders for medicines, timers for cooking, that sort of thing. But sometimes I find myself getting confused with all those different voices and commands. And the Wi-Fi connection is always dropping, which gets me frustrated! Still, it's nice having something like that to rely on, even if I don't fully grasp how it works itself.

I think prioritising is all about weighing what's most important, isn't it? For me, staying healthy and being able to spend quality time with my grandkids takes top spot. As a retiree, I feel like I'm in a lovely position where I have more freedom to pursue those kinds of relationships and experiences. Of course, that requires some juggling – balancing family duties with personal projects like tending to my garden and keeping myself occupied, so I make sure to set aside dedicated time each day for whatever comes first. If there are other demands vying for priority, I'll always try to assess whether they're urgent or essential. Like when I need to sort out paperwork or tackle maintenance issues around the house. Those take precedence because they impact daily routines and stability. But even amidst these busy periods, I find I can still squeeze in something creative – perhaps rearranging books in my local library's display case or experimenting with a new recipe for the kids. It helps keep me grounded and motivated!
